title: Housing Price Data Pipelining with AWS
text:
Step 03. ETL
When we want to process raw data stored in S3 (or other sources), clean it, transform it, and load it somewhere else (like S3, RDS, Redshift, DynamoDB), AWS gives us two options.

AWS Lambda: Good for lightweight, event-driven ETL
AWS Glue: Managed ETL service for larger workloads

In this project, we focus on executing large-scale ETL using AWS Glue. As the initial step, we build a Glue Data Catalog by running a Glue Crawler, which automatically infers table schemas from the raw data. To configure the Crawler, we add the data source by connecting to the S3 bucket through the AWS Console. The Crawler then scans the data, identifies the structure, and creates the corresponding table schemas and partitions.
A Glue Data Catalog is ready for efficient processing of large-scale datasets.



Crawler created a Data Catalog





Raw tables drawn from a Data Catalog


In short,

Source (to fetch): S3
Transform: SQL queries
Targets (to save): S3

Moving on to the ETL process, we use SQL queries to clean and standardize the raw data. This includes renaming columns, converting string values to numeric types (e.g., float or integer), removing unnecessary commas, and replacing null values with zero. The SQL queries used here are presented as below.


Show SQL Query

SELECT sggCd AS sgg_cd
     , case when sggCd = '11110' then 'AREA_01'
            when sggCd = '11140' then 'AREA_02'
            when sggCd = '11170' then 'AREA_03'
            when sggCd = '11200' then 'AREA_04'
            when sggCd = '11215' then 'AREA_05'
            when sggCd = '11230' then 'AREA_06'
            when sggCd = '11260' then 'AREA_07'
            when sggCd = '11290' then 'AREA_08'
            when sggCd = '11305' then 'AREA_09'
            when sggCd = '11320' then 'AREA_10'
            when sggCd = '11350' then 'AREA_11'
            when sggCd = '11380' then 'AREA_12'
            when sggCd = '11410' then 'AREA_13'
            when sggCd = '11440' then 'AREA_14'
            when sggCd = '11470' then 'AREA_15'
            when sggCd = '11500' then 'AREA_16'
            when sggCd = '11530' then 'AREA_17'
            when sggCd = '11545' then 'AREA_18'
            when sggCd = '11560' then 'AREA_19'
            when sggCd = '11590' then 'AREA_20'
            when sggCd = '11620' then 'AREA_21'
            when sggCd = '11650' then 'AREA_22'
            when sggCd = '11680' then 'AREA_23'
            when sggCd = '11710' then 'AREA_24'
            when sggCd = '11740' then 'AREA_25'
            else ''
       end as sgg_nm
     , umdNm as umd_nm
     , aptNm as apt_nm
     , jibun
     , cast(excluUseAr as float) as exclu_use_ar
     , cast(dealYear as int) as deal_year
     , cast(dealMonth as int) as deal_month
     , cast(dealDay as int) as deal_day
     , cast(replace(dealAmount, ',', '') as int) as deal_amount
     , cast(floor as int) as floor
     , cast(buildYear as int) as build_year
     , case when cdealType == ' ' then null
            else cdealType
       end as cdeal_type
     , case when cdealDay == ' ' then null
            else cdealDay
       end as cdeal_day
     , case when dealingGbn == ' ' then null
            else dealingGbn
       end as dealing_gbn
     , case when estateAgentSggNm == ' ' then null
            else estateAgentSggNm
       end as estate_agent_sgg_nm
     , case when rgstDate == ' ' then null
            else rgstDate
       end as rgst_date
     , case when aptDong == ' ' then null
            else aptDong
       end as apt_dong
     , case when slerGbn == ' ' then null
            else slerGbn
       end as sler_gbn
     , case when buyerGbn == ' ' then null
            else buyerGbn
       end as buyer_gbn
     , case when landLeaseholdGbn == ' ' then null
            else landLeaseholdGbn
       end as land_leasehold_gbn
     , deal_ymd
     , lawd_cd
  FROM apt-trade-raw
In AWS Athena, we can make SQL analysis directly on data stored in Amazon S3 without the need to set up a database or server infrastructure. Athena allows us to query structured, semi-structured, or raw data using standard SQL, making it a powerful tool for exploratory data analysis and validation after ETL processes.

title: Substation Data Pipelining with GCP
text:
The ongoing transition toward a climate-neutral energy system is accelerating the integration of decentralized renewable energy sources - often referred to as sector coupling - such as photovoltaic (PV) systems and wind turbines, along with the electrification of transportation through electric vehicles (EVs). Consequently, as described in Figure 01, current low-voltage (LV) grids are experiencing increasing operational stress due to bi-directional power flows and rising peak demand. In this evolving environment, distribution system operators (DSOs) are tasked with ensuring secure and reliable grid operation. Real-time monitoring of the grid state, combined with accurate assessment of peak load (energy consumption) and peak generation (energy injection), is therefore essential to prevent thermal overloading, mitigate voltage violations, and maintain system stability



Figure 01. Decentralized energy consumption and generation in low-voltage grids: It leads to bi-directional power flows - power no longer flows uni-directionally from the transformer to consumers -, increasing the risk of overloading and voltage instability.





Smart secondary substations such as digiONS provide real-time feeder cable monitoring, which significantly enhances system observability. However, substation-level monitoring systems with digiONS, particularly those based on SCADA infrastructure, are susceptible to measurement noise, incomplete sampling intervals, aggregation artifacts, and communication failures. These issues reduce the reliability of the data and make direct analytical use difficult. Consequently, systematic data acquisition control and quality assurance mechanisms are indispensable for maintaining the credibility of the LV grid analysis.

Goal: Our goal is to ensure the quality of data collected during the operation of the smart secondary substations. We design and implement a scalable data pipeline for operational substation monitoring data using a Medallion architecture (Bronze → Silver → Gold). The proposed pipeline is designed to:

ingest operational measurements from substation monitoring systems
perform data quality validation
transform raw data into analysis-ready datasets
provide monitoring dashboards and automated alerting mechanisms.






The empirical data used in this study are sourced from the publicly available Kaggle dataset: Power Exchange Data Germany. The dataset provides detailed operational measurements for one representative substation. The investigated substation supplies a LV grid consisting of 89 households, 450 kW of installed PV capacity, and a battery storage system rated at 300 kW / 850 kWh. The operational dataset collected from this substation contains active power measurements recorded at the LV busbar. These measurements are particularly useful for system analysis, as they reflect the combined effects of household demand, photovoltaic generation injected into the grid, and battery operation under bidirectional power flow conditions. In this chapter, these active power measurements serve as the primary basis for investigating data acquisition consistency and quality assurance aspects.



Figure 02. Substation-level Active Power measurements and sample counts.


A snapshot of the dataset is shown in Figure 02. Active power value is reported as 15-minute average values (kW), computed from underlying 1-minute measurements. In addition to the average value, the dataset includes the corresponding minimum and maximum 1-minute readings within each aggregation interval, as well as the number of valid samples collected from each 15-minute interval in the SCADA-based aggregation process. However, as discussed earlier, the dataset may contain data quality issues commonly associated with SCADA monitoring systems, such as incomplete sampling intervals, aggregation artifacts, and communication failures. The Available Data Count column shown in Figure 02. reflects this aspect by indicating the number of available measurements used in the aggregation of each interval. To examine these data quality issues, this study formulates eight assurance standards at the level of each 15-minute observation interval:

Missing timestamp: each record is checked for missing Timestamp value, which may arise from communication failures or related disruptions.
Missing available data count: each record is checked for the presence of the Available Data Count value, which may arise from communication failures or related disruptions.
Missing signal identifier: each record is checked for a valid signal identifier so that observations can be uniquely referenced.
Duplicate signal identifier: each record is checked to ensure that no duplicated signal identifier exists, so that observations can be uniquely referenced.
Timestamp misalignment: each Timestamp value is checked to confirm that it falls on a quarter-hour boundary (00, 15, 30, or 45 minutes), as expected for a stable 15-minute signal feed.

In addition to these structural issues, this study also considers consistency-related problems in the reported active power measurement statistics.

Invalid minimum-average relationship: the reported Active Power min value must not exceed the Active Power AVG value, in accordance with the expected ordering of aggregated statistics.
Invalid average-maximum relationship: the reported Active Power AVG value must not exceed the Active Power max value, since such a case would indicate inconsistency in the aggregated statistics.
Unexpected sample count: the aggregation window is checked for completeness by requiring the Available Data Count measurements within each 15-minute interval to equal 15.

Among these, unexpected sample count is particularly important, since deviations from this expected count may indicate irregularities in the SCADA-based acquisition process. Taken together, these quality issues provide a practical basis for judging whether a recorded interval is sufficiently reliable for subsequent analysis.











Metric
Target
Criteria




Completeness rate
≥ 99%
NO.valid intervals / 96


Timestamp Alignment
96
NO.aligned intervals [00 15 30 45]


Q-Test Pass Rate
≥ 95%
dbt test result


E[t] to Acknowledge
≤ 1 hour
As of the alert time


E[t] to Repair
≤ 12 hours
Turnaround time



(Each day contains 24 × 4 = 96 expected intervals)




Medallion Layer Implementation

Bronze: Raw CSV data are ingested using Apache Spark (append-only) + metadata (ingestion timestamp, batch identifiers, etc.)
Silver: Schema standardization (parsing timestamp, normalizing event types, applying conditional column rules, etc.)

validation rules:

active_power_min ≤ active_power_mean ≤ active_power_max
data_count ≤ 15
timestamp continuity check.


Gold: Curating data marts designed for analytical use

Data Mart for “Load Ratio Analysis”
Monitoring Table I. (daily KPI)

NO.valid intervals | NO.empty intervals | NO.partial intervals | NO.aligned intervals | completeness rate |

Monitoring Table II. (daily issue-type summary)

issue type | issue count | first issue ts | last issue ts |

Analytical Table

signal_id | signal_date | quarter_hour_slot | DataCNT | AVGsig | MINsig | MAXsig |

Derived Indicators

daily peak load
load variability metrics
power fluctuation statistics



Orchestration (Airflow)

Airflow orchestrates the transformation workflow within the Medallion architecture.

Schedule execution ⇒ Ingestion using Spark ⇒ dbt run(Silver/Gold) ⇒ dbt test ⇒ slack

In case of failure in any transformation or validation, the workflow automatically retries tasks and provides log links for troubleshooting.
Data Quality Monitoring (dbt test)

not_null, unique, accepted_values, relationships



Alerting (Slack)

Airflow ensures that each transformation stage is executed sequentially and that downstream tasks are only triggered when upstream validation checks succeed.

If DQ-tests fail, automated alerts are sent via Slack notifications.
The alerts include information (batch ID, affected tables, causes of the issue, etc.)
Rapid detection and resolution of data quality issues within the pipeline.


Scheduling

The transformation workflow is scheduled to execute on a daily basis to ensure that analytical datasets in the Gold layer remain consistent with the latest available operational measurements. Failure handling mechanisms include:

automatic retry attempts
task dependency validation
alert notifications to system operators.







SLA (service level agreement)

Monitoring and reporting must be available daily.
Gold Mart tables must be published before 7 am. with the data derived from the previous day’s records.
If dbt tests fail, the publication of Gold tables is blocked as a gatekeeping mechanism.

Performance & Cost

Daily pipeline execution should complete within 30 minutes.
Query costs are managed by using Google Cloud BigQuery table partitioning based on the event_date field.

Safety

Access to Bronze layer raw data should be restricted to authorized users.





Given that SCADA-based operational datasets are prone to measurement and communication errors, a structured set of data quality rules is implemented in the Silver layer to ensure reliability before the data are exposed for analytical use. The validation rules focus on four main dimensions: - Completeness, Validity, Consistency, Timeliness

Data quality monitoring is implemented through automated validation checks executed during pipeline execution.
If any rule fails:

the affected dataset is flagged
alerts are generated
Gold dataset publication may be temporarily blocked.








Figure 03. ETL data pipeline architecture for substation monitoring simulation. The architecture integrates streaming ingestion (Kafka and Spark), cloud-based landing storage (GCS), Medallion data modeling in BigQuery (Bronze-Silver-Gold), orchestration via Apache Airflow, and automatic visualization and alerting through Looker Studio and Slack.

Example of a Slack notification generated by the pipeline:







Note



rotating_light: Data Freshness SLA Violation
Dataset: Calendar Availability Batch ID: 2026-03-07-01 Affected Table: gold_calendar_availability
Issue: 3% of listings have not updated their calendars for more than 24 hours.
Impact: City-level availability metrics may be incomplete.
Suggested Action: Check upstream ingestion jobs and API synchronization logs.





Figure 03. Slack Notification.



Daily Monitoring Report includes:

pipeline execution status
dataset freshness
data quality test results
detected anomalies.






The pipeline will be continuously improved through:

periodic data quality & SLA compliance review.
A built-in review process ensures that data quality rules are validated whenever new tables are added.
Adjustment of validation rules.
Feedback-driven rule improvement: reducing false positives and improving alert precision
Enhancement of monitoring dashboards.
Optimization of pipeline performance.

≥ 99%

96

Q-Test Pass Rate
≥ 95%
dbt test result


E[t] to Acknowledge
≤ 1 hour
As of the alert time


E[t] to Repair
≤ 12 hours
Turnaround time



(Each day contains 24 × 4 = 96 expected intervals)




Medallion Layer Implementation

Bronze: Raw CSV data are ingested using Apache Spark (append-only) + metadata (ingestion timestamp, batch identifiers, etc.)
Silver: Schema standardization (parsing timestamp, normalizing event types, applying conditional column rules, etc.)

validation rules:

active_power_min ≤ active_power_mean ≤ active_power_max
data_count ≤ 15
timestamp continuity check.


Gold: Curating data marts designed for analytical use

Data Mart for “Load Ratio Analysis”
Monitoring Table I. (daily KPI)

NO.valid intervals | NO.empty intervals | NO.partial intervals | NO.aligned intervals | completeness rate |

Monitoring Table II. (daily issue-type summary)

issue type | issue count | first issue ts | last issue ts |

Analytical Table

signal_id | signal_date | quarter_hour_slot | DataCNT | AVGsig | MINsig | MAXsig |

Derived Indicators

daily peak load
load variability metrics
power fluctuation statistics



Orchestration (Airflow)

Airflow orchestrates the transformation workflow within the Medallion architecture.

Schedule execution ⇒ Ingestion using Spark ⇒ dbt run(Silver/Gold) ⇒ dbt test ⇒ slack

In case of failure in any transformation or validation, the workflow automatically retries tasks and provides log links for troubleshooting.
Data Quality Monitoring (dbt test)

not_null, unique, accepted_values, relationships



Alerting (Slack)

Airflow ensures that each transformation stage is executed sequentially and that downstream tasks are only triggered when upstream validation checks succeed.

If DQ-tests fail, automated alerts are sent via Slack notifications.
The alerts include information (batch ID, affected tables, causes of the issue, etc.)
Rapid detection and resolution of data quality issues within the pipeline.


Scheduling

The transformation workflow is scheduled to execute on a daily basis to ensure that analytical datasets in the Gold layer remain consistent with the latest available operational measurements. Failure handling mechanisms include:

automatic retry attempts
task dependency validation
alert notifications to system operators.







SLA (service level agreement)

Monitoring and reporting must be available daily.
Gold Mart tables must be published before 7 am. with the data derived from the previous day’s records.
If dbt tests fail, the publication of Gold tables is blocked as a gatekeeping mechanism.

Performance & Cost

Daily pipeline execution should complete within 30 minutes.
Query costs are managed by using Google Cloud BigQuery table partitioning based on the event_date field.

Safety

Access to Bronze layer raw data should be restricted to authorized users.





Given that SCADA-based operational datasets are prone to measurement and communication errors, a structured set of data quality rules is implemented in the Silver layer to ensure reliability before the data are exposed for analytical use. The validation rules focus on four main dimensions: - Completeness, Validity, Consistency, Timeliness

Next, let us consume the kafka message, using spark to store data points in a “parquet” format into GCS. For this, Go to GCP and create a bucket. Find “IAM” section, and find the “service accounts” on the left panel. Name “service-account-mk”, and choose the permission as “editor”. Access the “service-account-mk”, move to the “key” tab to fetch the new JSON key and put it in the “secrets” folder in your machine.
Now, submit spark job, using consume_kafka.py.






Note

docker compose exec spark /opt/spark/bin/spark-submit \ --master local[*] \ --packages org.apache.spark:spark-sql-kafka-0 10_2.12:3.5.1,com.google.cloud.bigdataoss:gcs-connector:hadoop3-2.2.19 \ /opt/spark/work-dir/consume_kafka.py


consume_kafka.py:


Code
####################################################################
# reads Kafka continuously
# writes to GCS as Parquet
# adds partition columns by processing time
# stays alive and prints a useful error if the stream fails
####################################################################

import time
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, date_format, col

# -----------------------------
# 1. Basic config
# -----------------------------
APP_NAME = "kafka-consumer"

BOOTSTRAP = "kafka:9092"
TOPIC = "signal_events"
GROUP_ID = "spark_signal_consumer"

GCS_PATH = "gs://test-kafka-mk/digiONS/raw"
GCS_CHECKPOINT = "gs://test-kafka-mk/checkpoints/digiONS"

GCP_KEYFILE = "/opt/spark/secrets/wired-effort-483112-k2-59425ba2002c.json"

# -----------------------------
# 2. SparkSession
# -----------------------------
spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .appName(APP_NAME)
    .config("spark.hadoop.fs.gs.impl", "com.google.cloud.hadoop.fs.gcs.GoogleHadoopFileSystem")
    .config("spark.hadoop.fs.AbstractFileSystem.gs.impl", "com.google.cloud.hadoop.fs.gcs.GoogleHadoopFS")
    .config("spark.hadoop.google.cloud.auth.service.account.enable", "true")
    .config("spark.hadoop.google.cloud.auth.service.account.json.keyfile", GCP_KEYFILE)
    .getOrCreate()
)

spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel("INFO")

print("Spark session created")
print("App name:", APP_NAME)
print("Kafka bootstrap:", BOOTSTRAP)
print("Kafka topic:", TOPIC)
print("GCS path:", GCS_PATH)
print("Checkpoint path:", GCS_CHECKPOINT)

# -----------------------------
# 3. Read Kafka stream
# -----------------------------
raw_df = (
    spark.readStream
    .format("kafka")
    .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", BOOTSTRAP)
    .option("subscribe", TOPIC)
    .option("startingOffsets", "earliest")
    .option("kafka.group.id", GROUP_ID)
    .option("failOnDataLoss", "false")
    .load()
)

# Keep useful Kafka metadata too
value_df = raw_df.selectExpr(
    "CAST(key AS STRING) AS kafka_key",
    "CAST(value AS STRING) AS value",
    "topic",
    "partition",
    "offset",
    "timestamp AS kafka_timestamp"
)

# -----------------------------
# 4. Add processing-time partitions
# -----------------------------
with_ts = (
    value_df
    .withColumn("proc_ts", current_timestamp())
    .withColumn("event_date", date_format(col("proc_ts"), "yyyy-MM-dd"))
    .withColumn("event_hour", date_format(col("proc_ts"), "HH"))
    .withColumn("event_minute", date_format(col("proc_ts"), "mm"))
)

# -----------------------------
# 5. Write stream to GCS
# -----------------------------
query = (
    with_ts.writeStream
    .format("parquet")
    .option("path", GCS_PATH)
    .option("checkpointLocation", GCS_CHECKPOINT)
    .outputMode("append")
    .partitionBy("event_date", "event_hour", "event_minute")
    .queryName("signals_to_gcs_partitioned")
    .start()
)

print("Streaming query started")
print("Query name:", query.name)
print("Query id:", query.id)
print("Query runId:", query.runId)

# -----------------------------
# 6. Keep alive and show status
# -----------------------------
try:
    while query.isActive:
        print("----- stream status -----")
        print(query.status)
        if query.lastProgress is not None:
            print("Last progress:", query.lastProgress)
        else:
            print("Last progress: None yet")
        time.sleep(10)

    # If stream becomes inactive unexpectedly, surface the reason
    if query.exception() is not None:
        raise Exception(query.exception())

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Stopping stream by user request...")
    query.stop()

except Exception as e:
    print("STREAM FAILED")
    print(str(e))
    raise

finally:
    print("Shutting down Spark session...")
    spark.stop()





Figure 05. Google cloude storage bucket as a datalake.





[C]. From Bronze to Silver Medallion

(1) Pick up your Bronze layer by creating BigQuery External Table
Let us identify the target GCS bucket first and the folder path that contains the Parquet files. Since Parquet is a self-describing format, BigQuery can infer the schema directly from the files, so an explicit schema definition is not strictly required.
A table definition file can then be created with the bq mkdef command, and that definition is used to create the external table with bq mk. In this way, the raw data remain physically stored in GCS, while BigQuery provides SQL access to them as an external table.
Go to the GCP and “activate the cloud shell”, and pick up the GCS bucket:






Note



minkun_kim4@cloudshell:~ (wired-effort-483112-k2)$
bq mkdef –source_format=PARQUET –hive_partitioning_mode=AUTO – hive_partitioning_source_uri_prefix=gs://test-kafka-mk/digiONS/raw/ gs://test-kafka mk/digiONS/raw/event_date=* > digiONSsignal_ext.def


And.. we create new dataset and table:






Note



minkun_kim4@cloudshell:~ (wired-effort-483112-k2)$ bq mk –dataset wired-effort-483112-k2:test
minkun_kim4@cloudshell:~ (wired-effort-483112-k2)$ bq mk –external_table_definition=digiONSsignal_ext.def wired-effort-483112-k2:test.digiONSsignal_ext


Now, we can see the schema-on-read table!



Figure 06. BigQuery External Table displayed via real-time query





(2) Silver layer and Silver validation test
From Silver, we need to parse this JSON, and give structure.



Figure 07. Parsing example



Go back to VS Code, construct the Silver layer and perform the Silver test:






Note



minkun@instance-20260311-173303:~/40-dbt$ docker exec -it dbt-core bash

Figure 08. dbt in action! Creating and testing the Silver layer.


During the execution, the eight data quality tests are subsequently performed on the standardized dataset. The data points that violate one or more of these tests are consolidated into a summary table, which is materialized and made available for inspection in the GCP BigQuery console.



Figure 09. Resulting tables of 8 DQ-tests. In the left-side panel, one can see that the Silver layer can be seen as successfully constructed.





[D]. From Silver to Gold Medallion

(1) Three Data Marts
In the Gold layer, the validated measurements (data points) from the Silver layer are aggregated and transformed into analysis-ready tables that can be queried directly without additional preprocessing. To implement this layer, three SQL models - fct_signal_clean_15min.sql, fct_signal_issue_daily.sql, fct_signal_quality_daily.sql, and one YAML file - gold.yml are created and placed in the gold folder under the models directory of the my_dbt_project.

fct_signal_quality_daily is a daily KPI monitoring table. It summarizes 15 minute signal intervals at the daily level. Under normal conditions, each day contains \(24 × 4 = 96\) expected intervals. The table reports how many of these intervals are valid, empty, partial, and correctly aligned each day, thereby serving as a daily data quality monitoring dashboard. This allows the KPI related to timestamp alignment to be identified quickly on a daily basis. By showing whether 15-minute intervals are recorded at the correct quarter-hour timestamps, it provides a daily indication of whether DigiONS is functioning properly in terms of timestamp alignment.
fct_signal_issue_daily is a daily issue-type summary monitoring table. It summarizes row-level signal-quality issues by day. The issue includes (1) Incomplete 15-minute Window, (2) Missing Minute-level Signals, (3) Delayed Ingestion, (4) Other Collection Problem. For a substation monitoring context, this helps quickly see whether the problem is occasional or persistent, whether it clusters in certain days or periods, how long the problematic window lasted each day, etc.
fct_signal_clean_15min is the clean analytical table for daily use. This table is not intended for monitoring; instead, it stores one trusted 15-minute substation interval per row after incomplete, null, misaligned, and internally inconsistent records have been removed. It is therefore suited to downstream analyses such as load-curve plotting, comparison of substation behavior over time, and other applications requiring reliable interval data. Accordingly, it preserves the original structure of the Power Exchange dataset while retaining only trusted records.

To creat these data mart tables, use the following:






Note



root@5231af4ae491:/usr/app/my_dbt_project# dbt run --select + fct_signal_quality_daily + fct_signal_issue_daily + fct_signal_clean_15min
root@5231af4ae491:/usr/app/my_dbt_project# dbt build

Figure 10. Resulting three tables of the Gold layer.




(2) Automating the Medallion pipeline
Now, we automate this pipeline across Bronze-Silver-Gold. Before we start, note the following:






Note



For airflow-30>.dbt - profiles.yml - .user.yml
For dbt-40>.dbt - profiles.yml - .user.yml


Both folders contain YAML files with the same name. The former is used for automatic execution through Airflow, whereas the latter is intended for the manual execution workflow described so far. The following Figure 11. shows the different configurations of these two YAML files, despite sharing the same filename.



Figure 11. YAML files for automatic and manual execution of the pipeline.


The following is our DAG files - dbt_signal_layered.py - to automate the pipeline execution.


Code
from datetime import datetime
import subprocess
import pendulum

from airflow.sdk import dag, task
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
from airflow.providers.slack.notifications.slack_webhook import send_slack_webhook_notification

GERMANY_TZ = pendulum.timezone("Europe/Berlin")

slack_fail_alert = send_slack_webhook_notification(
    slack_webhook_conn_id="slack_webhook",
    text=(
        ":red_circle: Airflow DAG failed.\n"
        "*DAG*: {{ dag.dag_id }}\n"
        "*Task*: {{ ti.task_id }}\n"
        "*Run ID*: {{ run_id }}\n"
        "*Logical Date*: {{ ds }}\n"
        "*Log URL*: {{ ti.log_url }}"
    ),
)

@dag(
    dag_id="dbt_signal_layered",
    start_date=datetime(2026, 3, 1, tzinfo=GERMANY_TZ),
    schedule="30 12 * * *",   # every day at 12:30 Europe/Berlin
    catchup=True,
    max_active_runs=3,
    tags=["dbt", "signal", "substation"],
    on_failure_callback=[slack_fail_alert],
)
def dbt_signal_layered():
    start = EmptyOperator(task_id="start")

    @task(task_id="run_bronze")
    def run_bronze():
        cmd = "cd /usr/app/my_dbt_project && dbt run --select tag:bronze"
        print(cmd)
        subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, shell=True)

    @task(task_id="run_silver")
    def run_silver():
        cmd = "cd /usr/app/my_dbt_project && dbt run --select tag:silver"
        print(cmd)
        subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, shell=True)

    @task(task_id="run_intermediate")
    def run_intermediate():
        cmd = "cd /usr/app/my_dbt_project && dbt run --select tag:intermediate"
        print(cmd)
        subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, shell=True)

    @task(task_id="run_gold")
    def run_gold():
        cmd = "cd /usr/app/my_dbt_project && dbt run --select tag:gold"
        print(cmd)
        subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, shell=True)

    @task(task_id="test_silver")
    def test_silver():
        cmd = "cd /usr/app/my_dbt_project && dbt test --select tag:silver"
        print(cmd)
        subprocess.run(cmd, check=True, shell=True)

    end = EmptyOperator(task_id="end")

    start >> run_bronze() >> run_silver() >> run_intermediate() >> run_gold() >> test_silver() >> end


dag = dbt_signal_layered()








Note

title: EU-Sustainability Reporting - E1
text:
Be transparent: In what way are you GREEN?
ESRS (EU Sustainability Reporting Standards) developed by EFRAG (EU Financial Reporting Advisory Group) provides mandatory reporting standards under the CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive) in the EU. ESRS define how companies must report on sustainability-related impacts, risks, and opportunities. They include two cross-cutting standards (ESRS 1 and ESRS 2) that explain the general principles, reporting structure, and required company-wide disclosures. The environmental standards (E1–E5) cover climate change, pollution, water and marine resources, biodiversity, and resource use and circular economy. The social standards (S1–S4) focus on a company’s own workforce, workers in the value chain, affected communities, and consumers. Finally, the governance standards (G1) addresses ethical business conduct, including corruption, lobbying, and transparency. ESRS operationalises DMA to assess both inward financial risk and outward environmental effect.

title: Double Materiality Assessment
text:
What is important for your sustainability reporting? While going through this, you will get to know your firm way deeper.
In sustainability reporting, there is no universal procedure that applies equally to all economic activities. Under ESRS, companies must first identify potentially relevant sustainability topics and then assess their relevance through a double materiality assessment (DMA).
In this process, the DMA acts as a filter that determines which sustainability topics are relevant and therefore require disclosure in accordance with ESRS II.

title: Double Materiality Assessment
text:
[A]. Phase 1 – Context analysis
It gives an overview of

firm’s activities, location, products
value chain (up/downstream interactions - supplier, firm, logistics, distributors, customers)
stakeholder (those influenced by decision)
others (regulatory landscape, industry analysis (checking partner firms), etc.)

According to the MAIG, AutoGroup GmbH should first prepare a context analysis. Key aspects of this analysis cover the firm’s activities and environment, the key stakeholders and the firm’s value chain.

(a) Activity analysis:

topic_1: strategy and business environment of the firm

The firm’s strategy focuses on maintaining a broad customer base, expanding electric-mobility services, and improving operational efficiency. The firm aims to strengthen its after-sales business, develop digital sales and service channels, and invest in EV-related capabilities such as charging infrastructure and high-voltage technician training. Sustainability and regulatory compliance are becoming increasingly integrated into strategic planning. The business environment is shaped by strong regulatory pressure (e.g., EU climate rules, environmental and safety regulations), the transition to electric vehicles, ongoing digitalisation of sales and workshop processes, competitive pressure from other dealerships and OEM-controlled sales channels, and a tight labour market for skilled mechanics. These trends create both risks (e.g., compliance costs, changing revenue mix) and opportunities (e.g., EV service growth, efficiency improvements).

topic_2: product/service portfolio of the firm

The firm offers a broad portfolio of automotive products and services. Its core activities include the sale of new and used vehicles across multiple brands, complemented by financing and leasing options for private customers and corporate fleets. The firm also provides trade-ins, vehicle delivery services, and tailored mobility solutions for business clients. A significant share of revenues comes from after-sales services, including maintenance, repairs, diagnostics, bodywork, and paint shop operations. The company is expanding its portfolio to include electric-mobility services such as EV charging infrastructure, battery diagnostics, and high-voltage system repairs. Additional services include spare parts sales, fleet management, and customer support across the vehicle life cycle, making AutoGroup GmbH a full-service mobility provider.

topic_3: geographic locations

The firm operates primarily in Germany, with a network of approximately 25 dealership and service locations distributed across several metropolitan and regional areas. These sites include showrooms, workshops, body shops, and administrative offices. While its core activities are domestic, the company interacts with international supply chains through vehicle manufacturers and parts suppliers. Its customer base is predominantly local and regional, with operations focused on serving private customers, corporate fleets, and mobility partners within Germany.


(b) Value chain analysis:

topic_1: Which stages of the value chain does the company control directly? Which are outsourced or partner-driven?

The following are directly controlled

Vehicle Sales: AutoGroup manages all new and used car retail activities at its own dealerships.
After-Sales Services: Workshops, inspections, diagnostics, and repairs are conducted by company employees on-site.
Body & Paint Operations: The company runs its own paint booths and bodywork facilities, handling associated processes internally.
Customer Service Processes: Customer interaction, consultation, and complaint handling are controlled directly.

The following are outsourced / partner-driven

Vehicle Manufacturing: All production is handled by OEMs, outside the company’s control.
Parts Supply & Logistics: OEMs and logistics partners manage upstream transport and inventory flows.
Financing & Leasing Services: Financial products are provided through banks or OEM financing units.
Waste Disposal & Specialized Training: Hazardous waste contractors and external trainers deliver required services


topic_2: main inputs (vehicles, parts, energy, water, data, labor)?

Vehicles & Parts: OEM-supplied cars and components form the foundation of the company’s sales and service business.
Skilled Labor: Mechanics, technicians, and sales staff are essential for both retail and workshop operations.
Energy & Water: Workshops, paint operations, and charging infrastructure rely heavily on electricity, heating, and water.
Chemicals & Data Systems: Paints, solvents, lubricants, and digital diagnostic data support core workshop processes.

topic_3: main outputs (products, services, waste, emissions)?

Vehicle Sales: New and used vehicles sold to private and corporate customers generate primary revenue.
Service Outputs: Repairs, maintenance, diagnostics, and bodywork services support ongoing customer needs.
Waste Streams: Workshops produce hazardous and non-hazardous wastes such as oils, chemicals, and tires.
Emissions & Energy Use Outcomes: Heating, electricity consumption, and workshop activities create operational emissions.

topic_4: geographic boundaries (Germany-only? EU imports? cross-border leasing)

Domestic Operations: All dealership and workshop activities take place within Germany.
International Supply Chains: Vehicles and parts originate from EU and global OEM manufacturing locations.
Cross-Border Dependencies: The company interacts with international financing or logistics partners even though operations are local.
Customer Market Boundaries: Sales and services focus exclusively on customers within Germany.


(c) Stakeholder analysis & evaluation:

topic_1: Who are the key stakeholders?

Customers (Private and Corporate)
Employees and Apprentices
OEMs and Suppliers
Regulators and Local Authorities

topic_2: **Relevance** 1. Interest

Customers (Private and Corporate): high-quality servicing, transparent pricing, and trustworthy customer support
Employees and Apprentices: fair working conditions, safe workplaces, competitive pay, and opportunities for training and development
OEMs and Suppliers: dealerships to meet brand standards, maintain sales volumes
Regulators and Local Authorities: full compliance with environmental, safety, and consumer protection regulations across all sites

topic_3: **Relevance** 2. Influence

Customers (Private and Corporate): shaping revenue and service demand
Employees and Apprentices: shaping service quality and customer satisfaction
OEMs and Suppliers: shaping product supply, technology development
Regulators and Local Authorities: imposing operational requirements

The stakeholder evaluation is performed to identify the Relevance of stakeholder group. - The relevance is determined by the highest value offered for the interest & influence.

The relevance is compared to the internal knowledge about the stakeholder. If the relevance exceeds the knowledge, it is translated into risk, and further engagement is required…via Survey, Interview, Workshop, SNS, etc.






Table 1. Example of Stakeholder Evaluation (high/medium/low) based on the Relevance of stakeholder groups.

title: Double Materiality Assessment
text:
[B]. Phase 2 – IRO identification
As a next step, the MAIG proposes the identification of IROs. As we expect that the E1 Standard might be material for the firm, we want to focus on potentially material IROs in this area first.

(a) IRO identification approaches:

In practice, there are different approaches for identifying IROs regarding the organizational levels.

Top-down: Derivation from the central level of the (Group) specialist department or the central sustainability task force. This approach provides a fast and potentially cost effective way to determine IROs, however it may miss out on feedback from subsidiaries.
Bottom-up: Identification takes place at subordinate levels such as individual subsidiaries or business units with subsequent consolidation at the overall company/group level. This approach potentially offers the broadest spectrum of potential IROs. However, this may result in additional work at group level to aggregate results.
Mixed approach: A combination of both approaches. This provides insights from both levels and may be useful in providing subsidiaries with initial anchors in terms of potentially relevant IROs. This approach likely is the most time-consuming and requires a lot of coordination.


(b) Sources to address E1 Standard:

E1. Sub topics:

Climate Change Mitigation
Climate Change Adaptation
Energy

The firm shall consider the list in ESRS I AR.16 (2023 ESRS) or ESRS I Appendix A (2025 ESRS). When performing its materiality assessment, the undertaking shall consider this list of sustainability matters covered in the topical ESRS. When, as a result of the undertaking’s materiality assessment (see ESRS II IRO-1), a given sustainability matter in this list is assessed to be material, the undertaking shall report according to the corresponding Disclosure Requirements of the relevant topical ESRS.
E1.Climate Change Mitigation

Q. In how far do the firm’s (or its value chain’s) actions contribute (positively/negatively) to climate change?

Negative impact: Sale of internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles contributes significantly to downstream Scope 3 greenhouse gas emissions from vehicle use.
Negative impact: Energy-intensive workshop, paint shop, and dealership operations generate operational (Scope 1 and 2) emissions.
Positive impact: Sale of electric vehicles (EVs) supports the reduction of transport-related GHG emissions.
Positive impact: Installation and operation of EV charging infrastructure enable the transition to low-emission mobility in the wider economy.

Q. In how far is the firm (or ist value chain) affected by the transition to a low-carbon economy, considering potential financial benefits or risks?

Transition risk: Decreasing demand and residual values for ICE vehicles due to stricter climate regulation and changing customer preferences.
Regulatory risk: Increased costs from tighter emissions regulations, reporting obligations, and carbon pricing affecting operations and fleet services.
Market risk: Potential loss of competitiveness if EV offerings, charging services, or low-carbon services lag behind competitors.
Market opportunity: Growing demand for EVs can increase revenues from vehicle sales and related services.
Business model opportunity: Expansion of EV charging infrastructure and e-mobility services for private and fleet customers.
Financial opportunity: Access to green financing and sustainability-linked funding due to alignment with decarbonization objectives.
Efficiency opportunity: Energy efficiency measures in dealerships and workshops can reduce operating costs and emissions simultaneously.


E1.Climate Change Adaptation

Q. In how far does the firm (or its value chain) adapt to actual/potential impacts of climate change?

Negative impact: Limited and uncoordinated adaptation measures at dealership sites may increase the vulnerability of employees, customers, and local communities during extreme heat, storms, or flooding events.
Positive impact: Existing basic resilience measures (e.g., climate-controlled workshops, stormwater management, asset protection) contribute to reduced climate-related damage and operational disruption.
Value chain impact: Disruptions at logistics partners, vehicle manufacturers, or fleet customers due to extreme weather can indirectly affect service continuity and customer reliability.

Q. In how far is the firm (or ist value chain) affected by climate change effects, considering potential financial benefits or risks?

Physical risk: Heatwaves may reduce employee productivity and increase health and safety risks in workshops and outdoor operations.
Physical risk: Flooding, storms, or heavy rainfall may damage dealership buildings, vehicle stock, charging infrastructure, and IT systems, leading to financial losses.
Operational risk: Climate-related disruptions at suppliers and transport partners may delay vehicle deliveries, spare parts availability, and fleet services.
Financial risk: Rising insurance premiums or reduced insurability due to increased physical climate risks at exposed locations.
Market opportunity: Growing demand for climate-resilient vehicles, fleet solutions, and related services due to more frequent extreme weather events.
Operational opportunity: Proactive investment in climate-resilient infrastructure reduces downtime, repair costs, and business interruption losses.
Service opportunity: Expansion of advisory and fleet management services focused on climate resilience and operational continuity.
Reputational opportunity: Positioning as a reliable and resilient mobility partner for corporate and fleet customers under changing climate conditions.


E1.Energy

Q. In how far do the firm’s (or its value chain’s) actions contribute (positively/negatively) to the consumption of energy?

Negative impact: High electricity and gas consumption from dealership buildings, workshops, paint shops, lighting, heating, IT systems, and charging infrastructure increases overall energy demand.
Negative impact (value chain): Energy use associated with vehicle preparation, logistics, and outsourced service providers adds to indirect energy consumption.
Positive impact: Implementation of energy-efficient equipment, building retrofits, and process optimization can reduce energy demand and related environmental pressures.
Positive impact: Potential on-site renewable energy generation (e.g., rooftop PV) can reduce reliance on external energy supply.

Q. In how far is the firm (or ist value chain) affected by its use of energy, considering potential financial benefits or risks?

Cost risk: Exposure to volatile electricity and gas prices increases operating costs and reduces profit margins.
Regulatory risk: Stricter energy efficiency standards and reporting requirements may lead to additional compliance and investment costs.
Operational risk: Energy supply disruptions or grid constraints may affect workshop operations, IT systems, and EV charging availability.
Transition-related risk: Increased pass-through of carbon costs in energy prices may further elevate energy expenses.
Efficiency opportunity: Energy efficiency measures (LED lighting, efficient compressors, heat recovery, smart building systems) reduce long-term operating costs.
Self-generation opportunity: Investment in on-site renewable energy (e.g., photovoltaic systems) lowers energy procurement costs and exposure to price volatility.
Flexibility opportunity: Smart charging and load management for EV infrastructure enable demand-side flexibility and potential grid service revenues.
Reputational opportunity: Demonstrating low and efficient energy use can strengthen the company’s sustainability profile with customers, OEM partners, and financiers.







Table 2. IRO Database.

title: Double Materiality Assessment
text:
[C]. Phase 3 – IRO Assessment
Equipped with a list of relevant IROs, let’s set up to assess their materiality.

Severity: Size of environmental effect (harm).
Extent: Size of financial effect (money).
Scope(moral dimension): How many people / How much environment is affected.
Irremediability(moral dimension): How reversible the harm is.
\(P(\text{Occurance}_i)\): probability the impact/risk/opportunity materialises.
(a) formulas used to calculate Impact Materiality scores (for Impact): \[
\begin{aligned}
\text{SCORE}_{I} = \text{Severity}_i &\times P(\text{Occurance}_i)  \quad \text{with   }P(\text{Occurance}_i)=1 \; \text{always for human right, } \\
& \text{where }\; \text{Severity}_i = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{2}\bigl(\text{Extent}_i + \text{Scope}_i\bigr),
& \text{if impact } i \text{ is positive}, \\[8pt]
\dfrac{1}{3}\bigl(\text{Extent}_i + \text{Scope}_i + \text{Irremediability}_i\bigr),
& \text{if impact } i \text{ is negative}.
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
\]
(b) formulas used to calculate Financial Materiality scores (for Risk/Opportunity): \[
\begin{aligned}
\text{SCORE}_{R
/O} = \text{Extent}_i &\times P(\text{Occurance}_i)   \\
& \text{where }\; \text{Extent}_i = \begin{cases}
\frac{\text{€ impact}}{\text{firm size}},
& \text{ how much money is at stake / how big the firm is}... \\[8pt]
\frac{\text{E[Financial impact]}}{\text{Reference Financial KPI}}
& \text{}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
\] Why Extent is expressed as a ratio? A certain loss can be huge for a small firm, and tiny for a large firm. So we scale the impact.

numerator: E[Financial impact] includes:

Expected revenue loss
Cost increase
Asset impairment
Regulatory fines

denominator: Reference Financial KPI (the firm’s relevant financial scale). Different risks hit different parts of the financial statements, so the denominator must match the economic channel of the risk. This includes:

Revenue
Operating profit
Asset values
Equity (shareholders’ capital)





How exactly is the threshold value - ranging from Extent: 1 to Extent: 5 - for risks/opportunities determined? it is sensible to adjust the thresholds according to the specific industry of the firm to adjust risks to the economic conditions of the business. In general, the following procedure has proved successful:

If risk management system exists: Use of the existing monetary threshold (80%, significance level of 0.2) for consideration as an anchor for medium (SCORE: 3)
Revenue 0.5% ~ 3.0% for medium (SCORE: 3)
Operating profit 3% ~ 10% for medium (SCORE: 3)


Again, if possible, try to orientate yourself on existing risk management methods.
So which topical standard is material? - In Extent, Scope, Irremediability, if any of them reaches the maximum, it becomes automatically material. - In Extent, Scope, Irremediability, if any of them imples the human rights, it becomes automatically material. - Finally, An overall SCORE above 2.5 is material.

title: Double Materiality Assessment
text:
[D]. Phase 4 – Reporting Data
After completing the identification and assessment of IROs, we prepare the data for reporting. In general, the firm is required to disclose as below:

In the final reporting step, the undertaking must disclose how the materiality assessment was carried out and what its outcomes are. This includes:

A description of the process to identify and assess material impacts, risks and opportunities (ESRS II IRO-1),
the material IROs identified and how they relate to the company’s strategy and business model (ESRS II SBM-3),
A clear indication of which topical ESRS disclosure requirements are covered in the sustainability statement (ESRS II IRO-2).
The undertaking must also explain how material information was determined, including the thresholds and criteria applied.

In addition, the company must report how its administrative, management and supervisory bodies (AMB) are informed about and oversee material sustainability matters. This includes how the AMB considers material IROs in the company’s strategy and risk management and how these matters were addressed during the reporting period.
